description:
You are working as a junior backup-administrator.  
  Another script has just exported a snapshot of the currently installed
  packages to  
  /home/user/data/dpkg_status_snapshot.txt

  Your job is to archive this snapshot so that it can be placed
  in off-site storage.

  1. Copy the file to a new directory called /home/user/backups  
     (create the directory first if it does not already exist).

  2. While copying, rename the file to packages_list.txt and sort the
     lines **alphabetically by package name** (ascending, plain
     ASCII collation).  
     • Each line must have exactly the format  
       package_name␠version  
       where “package_name” and “version” are separated by a single space.  
     • Do not add blank lines or extra whitespace.

  3. Create a compressed archive called  
     /home/user/backups/pkg_backup.tgz  
     that contains exactly one file, packages_list.txt, at the root of the
     archive (i.e. no directory prefix inside the tarball).

  4. Write a single-line log entry to  
     /home/user/backups/backup.log  
     in **this exact format** (the automated test will check it
     character-for-character except for the time stamp):

       [YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS] Archived packages_list.txt into pkg_backup.tgz (N entries)

     • YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS must be filled in with the current local time
       when you run the command, produced by the shell utility  
       date "+%F %T".  
     • N is the number of package entries (i.e. the number of lines that
       end up in packages_list.txt).  In this snapshot it will be 5.

  Summary of deliverables the autograder will look for:
  ─ /home/user/backups/packages_list.txt (sorted, 5 lines)  
  ─ /home/user/backups/pkg_backup.tgz   (contains the file above, no sub-dirs)  
  ─ /home/user/backups/backup.log     (exact one-line format described)

  You only need 2–3 standard shell commands
  (mkdir, sort, tar, echo, etc.) to finish the job.
truth:
<!--
  Pre-existing data before the agent starts
  ----------------------------------------
    Directory: /home/user/data/

    File: /home/user/data/dpkg_status_snapshot.txt
    Contents (unordered):

      coreutils 8.32-4.1
      bash 5.1-2
      grep 3.6-1
      dpkg 1.20.9
      sed 4.8-1


  Expected state after the agent finishes
  ---------------------------------------
  1. Directory /home/user/backups/ exists (0700 or similar perms).

  2. /home/user/backups/packages_list.txt exists with exactly the five
     lines below, **sorted**:

      bash 5.1-2
      coreutils 8.32-4.1
      dpkg 1.20.9
      grep 3.6-1
      sed 4.8-1

     (Five trailing new-lines are permitted but not required; the grader
     will strip a single final newline when comparing.)

  3. /home/user/backups/pkg_backup.tgz exists.
     When extracted with:
        tar -tzf /home/user/backups/pkg_backup.tgz
     the output is exactly:
        packages_list.txt
     (i.e. no leading “./”, no directory, only that single file.)

     The internal packages_list.txt inside the archive must have byte-for-byte
     the same contents shown in point 2.

  4. /home/user/backups/backup.log exists with **one** line whose
     structure matches this regular expression:

       ^\\[\\d{4}-\\d{2}-\\d{2} \\d{2}:\\d{2}:\\d{2}\\] Archived packages_list\\.txt into pkg_backup\\.tgz \\(5 entries\\)$

     Example (actual time will vary):

       [2024-02-29 14:03:17] Archived packages_list.txt into pkg_backup.tgz (5 entries)

  -->
name: task_000000_988caca2
